tax: Calcite Crust Elemental is placed at species rank beneath Chalk-Kin (Argillic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a flat spreading calcite sheet, crust-sealing attack, and a film that hardens over surfaces it has left. It is separated from its sibling the Tufa Column by its flat spreading sheet and sealing crust versus the Column's vertical hollow jet; the two share the genus body-plan but never the vertical jetting. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: thin precipitated calcite kept flexible by a film of supersaturated lime water.
ap: Calcite Crust Elemental stands indefinite creeping sheet, typically 0.3–0.5 cm thick. Its silhouette is a flat shimmering crust spreading across floor and wall. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a shimmering wet calcite film on surfaces that dries to a soft-clicking crust; a faint lime smell and a slight tackiness underfoot before the crust is visible; and a clicking sound as it advances, caused by micro-fracture and re-deposit. It moves with a creeping radial spread from a drip-point, up to 2 m per hour on wet stone, and shows a faint sheen on wet sections. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a white calcite film that hardens room-temperature over 6–8 hours, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
eco: Calcite Crust Elemental persists in still damp cavern floors and wall-faces fed by a persistent lime-heavy drip, where a flat body has no draught to disperse it. It does not breed; it forms where a lime-heavy drip animates a calcite nucleus into a spreading sheet, and recurs whenever the drip continues and the sheet regrows from the same nucleus. It sustains its mass on lime supersaturation in drip water, drawing in dissolved calcium from drip absorbed into the advancing edge. Range is bounded by the drip-fed damp zone: outside that band the body dries to hard inert crust off the wet stone. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a drip-point where calcite deposits faster than the drip alone could explain, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Calcite Crust Elemental is roused by warmth, breath, or moisture near its edge and otherwise lies a still crust coating the cavern floor, indistinguishable from natural flowstone. It claims a single drip-fed cavern floor and answers intrusion with spreading over gear, openings, and breathing faces to seal and suffocate. Toward grey-folk it is passive and slow, the danger is in not noticing it; survey crews report that Lanternkin test cavern floors with a blade-tip before resting; a tacky pulling sensation means live crust. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
traitsText: Stable characters of Calcite Crust Elemental: a body of thin precipitated calcite kept flexible by a film of supersaturated lime water; immune to piercing, which it re-covers, and to acid in dilute quantities; and a defining weakness — strong concentrated acid, which dissolves it faster than it repairs, and sudden heat. Its senses run through the medium itself: moisture and warmth sensing through its spreading edge. It tolerates complete darkness and low air but fails at strong acid and sudden heat. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the flat sheet form and clicking advance, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
abilities: Abilities of Calcite Crust Elemental are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it spreads over openings and gear, sealing and immobilising; it coats breathing surfaces to suffocate prey; it regrows from a drip-fed nucleus after scraping; and it thickens over time, armour-plating surfaces with hard crust. Each is a direct expression of lime supersaturation in a flat spreading calcite sheet, so it cannot, for instance, jet, stand upright, move fast, or cross dry stone. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
bg: In the Throat, Calcite Crust Elemental matters because it is the most commonly overlooked cavern elemental, and a tacky floor in a drip-cave is now the Lanternkin's first hazard check. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a sealing suffocation hazard logged with lime-drip cavern floors, and its presence reshapes routes through the still drip-caverns, now floor-tested and never rested on without scraping. A drip-point where calcite deposits faster than the water flow alone could explain marks a Calcite Crust nucleating.
g: Calcite Crust Elemental (Calcarius stratus) is a flat-spreading calcareous elemental that creeps as a thin calcite sheet and seals exits, lungs, and gear under lime crust. Documented from the still, damp cavern floors and wall-faces fed by lime-heavy drip, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a spreading sheet of thin calcite crust just thick enough to move and grasp — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Where the Tufa Column jets vertically, the Calcite Crust spreads invisibly — the flattest and least-noticed of the chalk kin. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a thin white calcite film hardening over every surface, gradually losing coherence and no carcass.
